ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226355009 maps to cooked rice as the closest parent concept; no Vietnamese plain steamed white rice specific variant code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of plain steamed white rice is approximately 180 g (one rice bowl), providing around 295 kcal and 45 g of carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Rice (Cơm Trắng) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plain Rice (Cơm Trắng), cr_id G00010577, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are preserved from the input record which cites HPB as the data source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice, cooked (jasmine/long-grain type), which is the closest approved analogous food to Vietnamese plain steamed white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 72 × 45.34 / 100 ≈ 32.6, rounded to 32.3 using 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100g × 180g serving / 100 = 45.34g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for a plain grain dish, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio approximately 0.7), consistent with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume plain rice without added salt or soy sauce and pair it with potassium-rich vegetables and lean proteins to maintain a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio per DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100g cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g provides limited attenuation of glycaemic response for a refined white rice product.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit plain white rice portions to no more than one-quarter of the plate per ADA plate method, substitute with lower-GI alternatives such as brown or red rice where possible, and always pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Plain white rice is low in saturated fat and cholesterol (0 mg/100g), but its high glycaemic index and refined carbohydrate content are associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed in large portions regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion sizes of plain white rice, favour whole-grain alternatives to reduce glycaemic load and triglyceride risk, and ensure the overall meal pattern is rich in vegetables, legumes, and unsaturated fats per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice has a high glycaemic load that may promote systemic inflammation; it lacks anti-inflammatory micronutrients such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, and antioxidants.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute plain white rice with whole-grain alternatives such as brown rice or quinoa and pair meals with anti-inflammatory foods including fatty fish, turmeric, ginger, and leafy green vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain Rice (Cơm Trắng) is steamed or boiled white rice that forms the cornerstone of Vietnamese cuisine and is consumed at virtually every main meal in Vietnam and across the Vietnamese diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The rice is prepared by washing polished white rice grains and cooking them with water until fully absorbed, yielding soft, slightly sticky, mildly fragrant grains with a neutral, subtly sweet flavour and a tender, cohesive texture. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrates with 3.7g dietary fibre and 0.21g sugar. Sodium is 231mg/100g, potassium 327mg/100g, calcium 54mg/100g, magnesium 40mg/100g, and iron 2.55mg/100g; cholesterol is 0mg. The glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. Plain rice is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, and serves as a neutral base for accompanying dishes such as grilled meats, braised fish, stir-fried vegetables, and soups. In clinical nutrition, it is classified as a refined carbohydrate with a high glycaemic load per standard serving, and whole-grain substitutes are recommended for metabolic health management.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010577) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-documented staple with an exact match in the approved primary regional source. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, applied as an analogous estimate for Vietnamese plain white rice.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this record (G00010577) as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; plain steamed white rice contains no animal-derived ingredients and cholesterol is confirmed absent. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (6.5), fat_g (4.21), and carbohydrate_g (25.19) are all non-zero. Sodium at 231mg/100g is notably elevated for plain rice and may reflect the HPB measurement of a prepared dish variant; consumers should be advised that home-cooked plain rice without added salt typically contains less than 5mg sodium per 100g. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice; no Vietnamese-specific GI measurement was identified in the approved source database. Glycemic load per serving (180g bowl) calculated as 72 × (25.19 × 1.8) / 100 = 72 × 45.34 / 100 ≈ 32.6 GL units.
